claim: Lupus-prone mice infected with curliproducing bacteria have lower autoantibody titers compared to controls.

result.claim: Lupus-prone mice infected with curliproducing bacteria have lower autoantibody titers compared to controls.
result.reasoning: The claim states that lupus-prone mice infected with curli-producing bacteria have lower autoantibody titers. However, multiple propositions from animal studies directly refute this. Specifically, proposition prop_130637 (Paper ID: 26084027) and prop_130651 (Paper ID: 302403530) both state that "Infection of lupus-prone mice with curli-producing bacteria triggered higher autoantibody titers compared to curli-deficient bacteria." These findings are consistent across two different propositions, both from animal studies with a credibility rating of 2.5/5, indicating strong evidence against the claim.

evidence.text: The immune system recognizes bacterial amyloid curli.

evidence.text: Both mice groups exhibited similar levels of bacterial colonization.

evidence.text: I.p. infections with curli-expressing S. Typhimurium or E. Coli Nissle accelerated onset in lupus-prone NZBxW/F1 mice.
evidence.source: AI-27 Bacterial amyloids from biofilms break tolerance in lupus

evidence.text: Systemic administration of curli/DNA complexes purified from Salmonella biofilms triggered autoantibodies production in non-predisposed mice.

evidence.text: Natural infections with curli-expressing S. Typhimurium triggered autoantibodies production in NZBxW/F1 mice.

evidence.text: sGsn−/− mice showed no signs of autoimmunity.

evidence.text: In the silica-triggered NZBWF1 autoimmune mouse model, feeding mice DHA at human equivalent doses of 2 to 5 g/d was strongly linked to the suppression of autoimmune markers such as autoantibody production.
evidence.source: Serum fatty acid profiles in systemic lupus erythematosus and patient reported outcomes: The Michigan Lupus Epidemiology & Surveillance (MILES) Program.
